coredata.dc:title: Novel supramolecular network of graphene quantum dots-vitamin B9-iron (III)-tannic acid complex for removal of chromium (VI) and malachite green
coredata.dc:creator: Mahmoud, Mohamed E.
Fekry, Nesma A.
Abdelfattah, Amir M.
coredata.dc:description: The present work is devoted to synthesize graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from waste sugarcane bagasse with average particle size 4.2 nm based on the TEM analysis. The produced GQDs were then aimed to combine with vitamin B9 (folic acid = FA) and Fe3+-tannic acid complex (Fe-TA) for the formation of a novel (GQDs@FA@Fe-TA) supramolecular network with average particle size 37.57 nm and pore diameter 38.35 nm. (GQDs@FA@Fe-TA) was evaluated as a multipurpose nanosorbent for removal of inorganic anionic (Cr(VI)) and organic cationic (malachite green, MG) pollutants from water. Cr(VI) removal was optimized as 99.5% (at pH 3.0 after 5 min), while 94.8% for MG (at pH 6.0 after 30 min). Adsorptions of both pollutants were exothermic and spontaneous and well explained with the pseudo-second order and Langmuir model. (GQDs@FA@Fe-TA) exhibited higher regeneration stability after five consecutive adsorption–desorption cycles (~2.1–4.3% loss). Finally, the potential applications of (GQDs@FA@Fe-TA) for removal of inorganic anionic and organic cationic pollutants from various water samples were 91.2–99.4% and 89.8–92.1% for Cr(VI) and MG, respectively.

head.ce:title: Novel supramolecular network of graphene quantum dots-vitamin B9-iron (III)-tannic acid complex for removal of chromium (VI) and malachite green

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Alexandria University, Alexandria, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Alexandria University, Alexandria, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work is devoted to synthesize graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from waste sugarcane bagasse with average particle size 4.2 nm based on the TEM analysis. The produced GQDs were then aimed to combine with vitamin B9 (folic acid = FA) and Fe3+-tannic acid complex (Fe-TA) for the formation of a novel (GQDs@FA@Fe-TA) supramolecular network with average particle size 37.57 nm and pore diameter 38.35 nm. (GQDs@FA@Fe-TA) was evaluated as a multipurpose nanosorbent for removal of inorganic anionic (Cr(VI)) and organic cationic (malachite green, MG) pollutants from water. Cr(VI) removal was optimized as 99.5% (at pH 3.0 after 5 min), while 94.8% for MG (at pH 6.0 after 30 min). Adsorptions of both pollutants were exothermic and spontaneous and well explained with the pseudo-second order and Langmuir model. (GQDs@FA@Fe-TA) exhibited higher regeneration stability after five consecutive adsorption–desorption cycles (~2.1–4.3% loss). Finally, the potential applications of (GQDs@FA@Fe-TA) for removal of inorganic anionic and organic cationic pollutants from various water samples were 91.2–99.4% and 89.8–92.1% for Cr(VI) and MG, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the first discovery of supramolecular structural chemistry in 1960 by C.J. Pedersen [1], the design, synthesis and applications of these materials have been rapidly expanded to contribute in different fields of adsorption, catalysis, sensing, energy and biomedicine [2]. Supramolecular structures, networks and adsorbents have been recently found great deals and applications in the last two decades in different fields as sensing, molecular imaging, metal extraction, drug formulation and drug delivery [3]. Supramolecular structure is mainly defined and focused on the presence of various molecules with noncovalent interactions and bindings in the forms of different forces as electrostatic, van der Waals, hydrogen bonding, coordinate bonding, hydrophobic, and some of these types of interactions may be cooperatively working in a single supramolecular complex [4]. The basic units in these structures are generally classified on the contribution of some polymers [5], crown ethers [6], cyclodextrins [7], calixarenes [8], cucurbiturils [9], pillararenes [10], and others [11–15]. The applications of supramolecular structures as extracting systems for inorganic cations and anions as well as some organic compounds as dyes, herbicides and pesticides, plant growing regulators and proteins were reported [16]. Supramolecular structures were also investigated as capturing materials for CO2, H2, CH4, C2H2 and other organic derivatives [17]. Adsorption techniques were also benefited from the features and advantages of supramolecular derivatives for removal of a number of toxic organic and inorganic pollutants [18–22].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are characterized by unique properties such as small diameter, low toxicity, inexpensive nature, simplicity of functionalization, eco-friendly and photo stability [23]. Several applications of GQDs were documented in various fields as drug delivery, storage devices, photocatalysis, bioimaging, biosensing, electrocatalysis and adsorbents for removal pollutants [24]. Several techniques were employed to produce GQDs from different organic materials such as sucrose, citric acid, glycerol and some agricultural wastes including laser ablation, pyrolysis, microwave irradiation, and hydrothermal with some evidences that the last one is the most efficient, simple and ecofriendly approach amongst others [25,26]. Examples of GQDs production from some agricultural wastes were recently reported from rice husk [27–30]. Moreover, the characteristics of GQDs including nanoscale dimensions, antifouling and antimicrobial properties recommended their potential for the construction of multi-functional composites in membrane formation and separation [31], removal of heavy metal via nanofiltration [32,33] and adsorptive removal of inorganic and organic pollutants from wastewater [34–37].

Sugar refining production is one of the oldest industries in Egypt as well as in other countries. This industry has been recently grown to produce 1.89 billion tons of sugarcane bagasse (SB) as a waste by-product in an annual basis with a production area of 27,000,000 ha spread over more than 100 countries [38]. Sugarcane bagasse is a heterogeneous lignocellulose containing 45–55%, 20–25%, 18–24%, 1–4% and 1% specified for cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, ash and waxes, respectively. Furthermore, bagasse includes plentiful oxygen and carbon elements compared to other agricultural wastes [39]. The advantage of bagasse is based on stability, cost-effectiveness and homogenous properties. Recently, a growing tendency towards more effective exploitation of agro-industrial wastes were reported [40]. Therefore, numerous routes and products were focused on the use of SB as raw materials for the production of ethanol, electricity, pulp, bio-fertilizer and paper production [41]. In addition, the valorization of bagasse has been utilized as a primal matter for fabrication of carbon-based materials [42].

Several important disadvantages of supramolecular structures were reported and related to possible aggregation in aqueous solution, pH limitations and recovery problems. These are seriously obstacle and restrict their direct applications in water treatment [43]. Therefore, it is essential to design and develop a new strategy and technology to overcome these problems, while preserving the merits and main skeleton of supramolecular structure. Therefore, the objective of this study is devoted to establish a pioneering material from the direct combination and functionalization of Fe3+-tannic acid complex (Fe-TA) and vitamin B9 (folic acid = FA) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the formation of (GQDs@FA@Fe-TA) as a novel supramolecular structure. The incorporated GQDs were prepared by the conversion of sugarcane bagasse as a low cost source via a green hydrothermal method. The presence of good and variable functionality in the assembled (GQDs@FA@Fe-TA) supramolecular structure will afford and provide an efficient material with acceptable porosity for possible interaction and binding with different organic and inorganic pollutants as well as cationic and anionic moieties. This will afford a good strategy to satisfy the recent high demands for obtaining a new efficient and valid approach for multi-pollutants removal from wastewater. Therefore, the assembled and characterized (GQDs@FA@Fe-TA) supramolecular structure in this work is aimed to investigate in adsorptive removal of inorganic anionic (Cr(VI)) and organic cationic (malachite green, MG) pollutants from water under various optimized experimental conditions.

Both chemicals and characterization techniques with their specifications are listed in Table 1
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs from sugarcane bagasse(SB)
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were prepared via treatment of sugarcane bagasse by hydrothermal method [44] . First, 4.0 g of crushed sugarcane bagasse (SB) was dispersed into 50 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 30 min. Then, this mixture was put into a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 5 h. When the reaction was completed, the vessel was cooled under air-conditioned. The supernatant solution was centrifuged to isolate insoluble materials and the yellowish-orange solution was collected to analyze the GQDs by UV–Vis spectrophotometry at λmax = 265 nm because of π-π* and n- π* transitions for (CC) and (CO), respectively. The Maximum emission of GQDs was accomplished at 428 nm, when the λexcitation was 340 nm by using photoluminescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of supramolecular network of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA
ce:section.ce:para: Supramolecular network GQDs@FA@Fe-TA was synthesized by one-pot covalent bond building. Briefly, 0.6 g of folic acid (FA) was added to a 1:1 mixture (DMSO: water) with stirring for 20 min then, 5.0 g of GQDs and 4.0 g of tannic acid (TA) were added to FA solution. After 30 min, the resulting mixture was neutralized (pH 7.0) via addition of diluted NaOH solution. Afterward, the solution of ferric chloride (FeCl3, 5.0 g / 50 mL) was added dropwise with vigorous stirring. Finally, the obtained precipitate was dried at 60 °C.

Adsorption technique was employed to optimize all experimental factors impacting the percentage removal (%R) of Cr(VI) and MG dye onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network. These include pH, time, mass, concentration, temperature, ionic strength and interfering ions.

At the pH factor, 10 mg of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network was shaken with 10 mL of MG (10 mg L-1) for 30 min or Cr(VI) (15 mg L-1) for 5 min at various adjusted pHs from 1 to 7. After the adsorption operation, the GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network was filtrated and the remaining amounts of MG or Cr(VI) were measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The %R was calculated from Eq. 1 (Table 2 ).

The point of zero charge (pHpzc) of the GQDs@FA@Fe-TA was acquired according to a previously reported method [23]. In this experiment, pHinitial sequences ranging from 2 to 10 were arranged by mixing (0.1 mol L-1) hydrochloric acid or (0.1 mol L-1) sodium hydroxide with 2 g of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA and 50 mL of NaCl (0.01 mol L-1). These mixtures were shaken for 4 h and the pHfinal values were measured after 24 h. The variation in pH (pHfinal - pHinitial) was plotted against pHinitial to calculate and identify the pHpzc of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network.

At the time factor, the reaction time was varied from 1 to 30 min at optimum pH (pH 3.0 and pH 6.0 for Cr(VI) and MG, respectively) for each pollutant. 10 mg of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network was mixed with 10 mL of MG (10 mg L-1) or Cr(VI) (15 mg L-1) and the remaining steps were completed as described above.

At the mass factor, GQDs@FA@Fe-TA masses were changed from 1 to 10 mg at optimum time and pH (5 min and pH 3.0 for Cr(VI), while 30 min and pH 6.0 for MG). The selected mass of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network was mixed with 10 mL of MG (10 mg L-1) or Cr(VI) (15 mg L-1) and the remaining steps were completed as described above.

At the concentration factor, the selected mass of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA was mixed with different concentrations of Cr(VI) and MG dye pollutants (5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 60, 80 and 100 mg L-1) under the optimum condition of pH, mass and reaction time (pH 3.0, 10 mg and 5 min for Cr(VI), while pH 6.0, 10 mg and 30 min for MG). The remaining steps were completed as described above and the adsorption isotherm models were then applied.

At the temperature factor, the adsorption process was applied at different temperature (298, 308, 328 and 348 K) at optimum conditions of pH, mass and reaction time (pH 3.0, 10 mg and 5 min for Cr(VI), while pH 6.0, 10 mg and 30 min for MG). The remaining steps were completed as described above and the collected data were employed to compute the thermodynamic parameters.

At the interfering ions factor, 100 mg of various salts (NaCl, KCl, NH4Cl, CaCl2 and MgSO4) were added to 10 mL of MG (10 mg L-1) or Cr(VI) (15 mg L-1) at optimum conditions of pH, mass and reaction time (pH 3.0, 10 mg and 5 min for Cr(VI), while pH 6.0, 10 mg and 30 min for MG) and the remaining steps were completed as described above.

At the ionic strength factor, different masses of NaCl (10, 30, 50 and 100 mg) were added to 10 mL of MG (10 mg L-1) or Cr(VI) (15 mg L-1) at the optimum conditions of pH, mass and reaction time (pH 3.0, 10 mg and 5 min for Cr(VI), while pH 6.0, 10 mg and 30 min for MG) and the remaining steps were completed as described above. The %R values of both pollutants in presence of different ionic strength of NaCl were calculated from Eq. 1.

Adsorptive removal of spiked MG dye and Cr(VI) pollutants from tap water, sea water and wastewater samples by the investigated GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network was also explored in this study. The removal experiments were accomplished by addition of 30 mg of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA to 50 mL of different samples spiked with MG solution (10 mg L-1) or Cr(VI) (15 mg L-1). The reaction mixture was shaken for 5 min (Cr(VI)) or 30 min (MG). Finally, the remaining unextracted Cr(VI) or (MG) in all samples were collected by filtration and the removal percentages of the two pollutants were elaborated from Eq. 1 as given in Table 2.

Regeneration of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network after initial use was accomplished by separate treatment with 15 mg L-1 Cr(VI) and 10 mg L-1 (MG) until equilibrium was reached and the percentage removals of Cr(VI) and MG were then calculated. The GQs@FA@Fe-TA-bound-Cr(VI) and GQDs@FA@Fe-TA-bound-MG were separated from the solutions, rinsed with deionized water and shaken with 50 mL of 0.1 mol L-1 hydrochloric acid for 1 h. The sample was then rinsed with water till pH 7.0 and dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight. The above steps were repeated five times to evaluate the removal efficiency of recycled GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network after five regeneration cycles.

GQDs were synthesized from sugarcane bagasse as a type of waste biomass and the structure of GQDs was confirmed by the FT-IR analysis ( Fig. 1
a). The broad band at 3512 cm−1 was presumed to indicate the existence of O-H stretching to confirm the presence of abundant oxygen groups in GQDs [27] . The peak at 2910 cm−1 is associated with C-H of methyl or methylene groups and the prominent peak at 1643 cm−1 belongs to conjugated and/or CC merged with CO [45] . The 1580, 1394, 1285 and 1120 cm−1 peaks are related to NH, CC, COC, CO; respectively [46] . The FT-IR image of folic acid is shown in Figure (1b). The broad peak in the range 3111–3542 cm−1 is ascribed to OH in glutamic acid and NH group of pteridine ring stretching [47] . Other characteristics peaks are evident as (CH) at 2927 cm−1, (CO) at 1694 cm−1 and (NH) at 1570 cm−1. The stretching vibration of (CC) for phenyl and pteridine ring at 1484 cm−1 and stretching (CO) of pteridine ring at 1188 cm−1 [48]. The spectrum of tannic acid is presented in Fig. 1c with the broad band at 3499 cm−1 attributed to OH. The sharp peak at 1713 cm−1 is due to CO and the peak at 1613 cm−1 is related to the aromatic (CC) bond. Three peaks at 1320, 1202 and 1029 cm−1 are assigned to symmetric (CO), (OH) of phenolic and asymmetric vibration (CO), respectively [49] . When the FT-IR spectrum of Fe-TA was compared with the spectrum of TA, the peaks of phenolic (OH) at 1332, 1197 and 1069 cm−1 in the Fe-TA were found more broadened than TA peaks (1320, 1202 and 1029 cm−1). Such changes can be used to confirm the interaction of hydroxyl groups in tannic acid with ferric ions for the formation of a supramolecular network ( Fig. 1 d). In addition, Fig. 1 e refers to a number of peaks at 3338–3517 cm−1 (OH and NH) which appear in the spectrum of FA and GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network indicating the presence of FA in the final product. When the spectrum of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA was compared with the spectra of FA and Fe-TA, reveals that the peak at 1666 cm−1 is sharper than the peaks in FA (1694 cm−1) and Fe-TA (1615 cm−1). The peak at 1577 cm−1 appears in the spectrum of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network indicates the presence of GQDs and FA within the structure of assembled material. Finally, the possible chemical arrangement of different components in GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network is represented in Scheme 1
.

Fig. 2
a represents the EDX of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network and it is clearly confirming the existence of C, N, O and Fe elements in the designed material with the percentages (wt %) 41.13%, 4.91%, 48.95% and 5.01%, respectively to refer to high number of active sites and functional groups that are endowed to GQDs@FA@Fe-TA for enhancing pollutants removal. In addition, Fig. 2 b shows the TEM image of fabricated GQDs. This image clearly indicates that GQDs are existing in small nanoparticle size (<10 nm), quasi spherical shape and mono-dispersed. The average particle size of GQDs was measured as 4.2 ± 0.19 nm by size distribution ( Fig. 2 b). Fig. 2 c shows the TEM image of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network and it is clear that GQDs are well dispersed in the template of supramolecular and interlinked with the folic acid chain and tannic acid. Also, it explains that the GQDs are arranged in the surrounding area of Fe-TA. Fig. 2 d shows the SEM image and particles of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network in irregularly forms, spherical shape and uniform distribution with 37.57 nm average particle size.

The surface area of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network was measured by BJH method. The surface area was 13.28 m2 g−1. The pore volume and size play a central role in the adsorption performance of any sorbent. The pore diameter of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA equals 38.35 nm which is wide enough to adsorb different contaminants.

The thermal stability of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network was explored from the TGA analysis ( Fig. 3
a). The thermal decomposition of this material was occurred as one step from 44.2 to 696.7 °C. The weight loss may be due to the partial decomposition of GQDs, FA and Fe-TA from this material. The XRD patterns of GQDs and GQDs@FA@Fe-TA are shown in Fig. 3 b. The characteristic peak at 24.89° is due to the graphitic structure in GQDs [50] . The peaks in GQDs@FA@Fe-TA are weak and less broad, while the peak at 44.84° refers to the loading of folic acid in the structure of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network. Moreover, the characteristic patterns of Fe-TA are detected at 2ϴ = 20° − 30° range, revealing the amorphous structure [51] .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sorption study of Cr(VI) and MG onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network

ce:section.ce:para:
In the adsorption process, the pH is a significant factor that can instantaneously affect the ionization degree of adsorbates and surface charge of adsorbents [23,27]. As represented in Fig. 4
a, the removal of Cr(VI) by GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network at fixed concentration (15 mg L-1) was confirmed as a pH-dependent with superb accomplishment at pH 3.0 (99.5%),. However, as the pH escalated to 7.0, the capture of Cr(VI) by GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network decreased to reach 77.2% after 5 min. The different Cr(VI) species in solution exist as soluble oxyanions (HCrO4 - and Cr2O7 2-) from pH 1.0 to 6.0, and CrO4 2- up to pH 7.0 [52,53]. At pH 1–3, the Cr(VI) predominant species is HCrO4 -, which can be easily adsorbed with lower free energy of adsorption. In addition, the pHpzc of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network was measured and found 3.1, revealing that the charge on GQDs@FA@Fe-TA surface is positive at pH < 3.1, and negative above pH 3.1. At pH < pHpzc, the active functional groups on GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network become protonated and thus high electrostatic attraction forces are favored between (HCrO4 - and Cr2O7 2-) and the surface of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA ( Fig. 4 a). However, the removal of Cr(VI) decreased noticeably with rising pH from 3.1 to 7.0 due to the electrostatic repulsion of negatively charged GQDs@FA@Fe-TA and anionic species of Cr(VI).

Moreover, Fig. 4 a represents the pH impact on the removal efficiency of MG dye by GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network. It is evident that the removal process of MG dye was gradually increased over a wide pH range 2.0–7.0 due to the cationic nature of this dye ionized which undergoes favorable interaction and binding with the anionic surface of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network [54] . The maximum uptake was established at pH 6.0 (94.8% for 10 mg L-1) after 30 min. Therefore, the collected results from the pH factor confirm that the assembled GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network may be employed as an efficacious sorbent for removal organic and inorganic pollutants as well as anionic and cationic species.

The dosage impact of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network on the uptake of MG and Cr(VI) ( Fig. 4 b) and the percentage removal values of the two adsorbates were soared from 87.9% to 94.8% and 46.4% to 99.5%, respectively, as the mass increased from 1 to 10 mg. This could be revealing to the increase in GQDs@FA@Fe-TA dosage and hence more surface area with more active sites were increased [23] . However, the removal of Cr(VI) didn’t demonstrate a substantial difference as the dosage increased from 5 to 10 mg (Fig. 4b) due to the rapid reaching to the equilibrium and saturation conditions. This refers to the presence of comparatively more binding and reaction sites onto the GQDs@FA@Fe-TA surface. On the other side, the increases in MG uptake were slow down with the increase in GQDs@FA@Fe-TA dosage due to the relative decline of MG binding with the active sites onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA surface.

Time-dependent study provides significant information on the removal rate of chromium (VI) and malachite green pollutants by the investigated GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network as illustrated in Fig. 4 c. In the initial step, capturing processes of chromium (VI) and malachite green were increased to reach 99.5% and 90%, respectively to the ultimate uptakes at 10 min. The second step was established after that to slow down the adsorption processes of chromium (VI) and malachite green pollutants by the investigated GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network as a result of repulsion forces between the interacting MG or Cr(VI) and surface. Such trend was found to produce the equilibrium and saturation conditions at 30 and 10 min, respectively [23,27].

Adsorption kinetics display a great contribution role on the physisorption and/or chemisorption features of materials [55]. In this study, four related kinetics models to Elovich, pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order and intraparticle diffusion were applied to identify the best appropriated for the experimental data. The pseudo-first order was utilized to depict physisorption process including dispersion and mass transfer of the adsorbate, whereas pseudo-second order characterizes controlled chemisorption [56,57]. The Elovich model describes the chemical nature of the adsorption operation which gives information about the relationship between the surface coverage magnitude and activation energy for chemisorption [58] . The intraparticle diffusion is considered the rate determining step (RDS) when using the relation between qt and t0.5 and passes by origin. The intercept C refers to the surrounding layer on the adsorbent [59].

The listed data in Table 3 reveals that capturing of both MG and Cr(VI) by GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network was best tailored to the pseudo-second-order model according to the computed R2 = 1.00 and 0.999 and qe(exp) = 14.9 and 9.48 mg g−1 for chromium(VI) and malachite green, respectively. Furthermore, the qe(cal) values of both pollutants were very close to the qe(exp) values to confirm the fitting of results with pseudo-second-order. The qe(cal) of Cr(VI) (14.9 mg g−1) was greater than that of MG (9.52 mg g−1) to indicate GQDs@FA@Fe-TA exhibited superior performance towards Cr(VI) than MG. These provide clear indicatives that the RDS is a chemisorption process including complexation, electron sharing and/or ion exchange mechanism between the two pollutants (Cr(VI) and MG) and the active surface functional groups and sites on GQDs@FA@Fe-TA [59]. Furthermore, the intraparticle diffusion plots for the adsorption of MG and Cr(VI) pollutants onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network could be interpreted into two successive steps (Fig. 4d). The 1st step in this model is referring to the diffusion of pollutant from bulk solution to the surrounding film surrounding the GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network. The 2nd step is related to the dispersion of adsorbate molecules into the micropores and macrospores of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network. The computed R2 values for uptake Cr(VI) and MG onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA were characterized to correspond to 0.548 and 0.978, respectively to refer to less fitting with this model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ionic strength factor and impact of co-existing salts
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 e shows the impact of ionic strength by the addition of different concentrations of sodium chloride to the adsorption reaction containing adsorbent and the adsorbate (Cr(VI) and MG). In case of Cr(VI), increasing the ionic strength from 10 to 100 mg was found to cause a significant decrease in Cr(VI) removal. This behavior is mainly based on the increase in chloride ions concentration which compete with anions of Cr(VI) for the same adsorption functional groups and sites on GQs@FA@Fe-TA. In case of MG, the % removal of MG increased with the increase in ionic strength due to partial neutralization of positively charges onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA surface. However, the presence of Cl- ions improved the removal efficiency of MG by the formation of ion-pairs with MG and thus the repulsion forces between MG and GQDs@FA@Fe-TA surface was decreased [60].

The batch experiments were carried out to investigate the individual effects of co-existing salts with Cr(VI) and MG. The coexistence of NaCl, KCl, NH4Cl, CaCl2, and MgSO4 salts with the adsorbates (Cr(VI) and MG) were found to exhibit no effect on their adsorption onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network as displayed in Fig. 4 f. However, the removal percentages of MG were slightly decreased in presence NH4Cl, CaCl2, and MgSO4 as compared to those identified percentages in their absence. While %R of Cr(VI) were slightly decreased in presence of only CaCl2. The reasons for this is related to the adsorption dependence of ions on their radius whereas the cations with smaller radii (Na+, K+, NH4 +, Ca2+ and Mg2+) are in favor of better ion-exchange with GQDs@FA@Fe-TA surface rather than other large size of adsorbates as Cr(VI) and MG. Moreover, Cl- anion was found to exhibit similar behaviors for decreasing the removal percentages by competing with anionic forms of Cr(VI) [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Initial concentration factor and adsorption isotherms
ce:section.ce:para: The results of initial concentrations of both pollutants on the efficiency of their removals and uptake by GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network confirmed a decreasing order in the removal percentages with elevation of the concentration. The decreasing in %R of both pollutants may be accredited to the active sites occupation of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA by the pollutant. In this respect, the adsorption isotherms (Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin and D-R) were also utilized to explain the equilibrium and provide valuable information about the reaction mechanisms between the two investigated adsorbates (Cr(VI) and MG) and GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network surface.

According to the Langmuir model, the sorption mechanism is homogeneous, with enthalpies and activation energies of adsorption specialized for each molecule. All active sites ought to have equivalent attraction towards the pollutant, So no migration on the surface of adsorbent [62,63]. Furthermore, the Freundlich model supposed that the sorption process is due to multilayer formation. The heat of sorption and interactions do not need to be evenly distributed on the heterogeneous surface [63]. in addition, the exponential distribution and energies of the active sites describe the heterogeneity of the sorbent surface. The D-R is utilized for describing the mechanism of adsorption onto the heterogeneous surfaces [64]. D-R is based on a semi-empirical equation result indicating that the mechanism of these adsorption models is dependent on pore filling. Furthermore, the D-R postulation is a multilayer character that includes Van der Waal's forces and has the potential to be applied to physical adsorption processes [65]. The Temkin model includes the reaction between the sorbent and the sorbate with neglecting of the concentration values and depending on temperature [63].

As represented in Table 4 and Fig. 5 a and 4b, the Langmuir model provided the best fits with high R2 value compared to other models. The maximum monolayer sorption capacity for GQDs@FA@Fe-TA was 107.2 mg.g−1 for MG at 30 min and 74.0 mg g−1 for Cr(VI) at 5 min. The Langmuir supposes monolayer encasement on homogenously dispersion [66], implying that chemisorption could take place in the adsorption processes of Cr(VI) and MG onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA. These results confirm that the adsorptions onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA were established by a mono-layer adsorption process, due to the existence of functional groups on the surface.

In Freundlich model, the values of nF were 3.56 and 1.94 for chromium(VI) and malachite green, respectively to refer to possible adsorption favorability of both pollutants onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA [67]. In Temkin model, the achieved R2 values were 0.956 and 0.972 for MG and Cr(VI), respectively. It may be concluded that chromium(VI) has a better adsorption efficiency than MG. The high values of bT point out to the existence of strong interaction between adsorbate and adsorbent [68]. The bT value of Cr(VI) was 262.1 J mol-1 and 145.2 J mol-1 for MG. These denote to a weak interaction between Cr(VI) or MG and GQDs@FA@Fe-TA. Consequently, the Temkin model was not followed in this work. The computed Qmax values from D-R model were corresponding to 72.1 and 65.1 mg.g−1 for malachite green and chromium (VI), respectively, while the E values were 0.0175 and 0.0372 KJ. Mol−1 for malachite green and chromium (VI), respectively for describing physisorption process (E < 8 KJ. Mol−1) [69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption temperature factor and thermodynamic parameters
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of temperature was studied at different heating condition (298, 308, 328 and 348 K). The experimental data revealed that the %R of both pollutants onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA were slightly decreased with increasing the temperature. As T increased from 298 to 348 K, the %R decreased from 94.8% to 92.7% and 99.3% to 99.0% for malachite green and chromium (VI), respectively. The spontaneity and capture energeties of Cr(VI) and MG on GQDs@FA@Fe-TA were also determined (ΔGo, ΔHo and ΔSo) [70]. The values of ΔHo and ΔSo were obtained from the relation between ln KD and 1/T. In table 5 , It is evident that the values of ΔGo were negative at all T, revealing that the adsorption of malachite green and chromium (VI) onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA is spontaneous. The values of ΔGo were −12.4 KJ.mol−1 (298 K) and −13.4 KJ.mol−1(348 K) for Cr(VI), while −7.2 KJ.mol−1 (298 K) and −7.4 KJ.mol−1(348 K) for MG dye. According to the outlined data, the decreasing of ΔGo with increasing T confirms better adsorption at high T. The values of ΔHo were −6.40 (Cr(VI)) and −6.31 (MG) KJ.mol−1, reflecting that the capture of both pollutants by GQDs@FA@Fe-TA was exothermic behavior. The ΔSo values were found positive indicating that more randomness of both pollutants during adsorption onto GQDs@FA@Fe-TA [71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network in removal of chromium (VI) and malachite green pollutants from real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Adsorptive removal of spiked MG dye and Cr(VI) pollutants from tap water, sea water and wastewater samples by the investigated GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network was also explored in this study. The removal experiments were accomplished by addition of 30 mg of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA to 50 mL of different samples spiked with MG solution or Cr(VI) then shaking for 30 min and 5 min, respectively. The removal percentages were 92.2% (from sea water), 99.4% (from tap water) and 91.2% (from wastewater) for Cr(VI) while 90.5% (from sea water), 92.1% (from tap water) and 89.8% (from wastewater) for MG after second run. Thus, the assembled GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network could be categorized as an effective adsorbent for the removal of chromium(VI) and malachite green from different water. The main advantages of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA for the removal of inorganic and organic pollutants from tap water, sea water and wastewater involve a short adsorption time and high efficacy.

Evaluation of desorption process can contribute to a better conception of the adsorption process and the viability of regenerating of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network. Hydrochloric acid solution (0.1 mol L-1) was utilized as the reagent in the desorption procedures of already adsorbed Cr(VI) and MG. The regenerated GQDs@FA@Fe-TA was then washed, dried and used in successive uptake of chromium (VI) and malachite green from solution. The impact of five consecutive regeneration cycles on the removal of both pollutants by GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network was deliberated. The recycled GQDs@FA@Fe-TA was found highly effective for adsorption of Cr(VI) and MG. The %R of chromium (VI) and malachite green by the recycled Cr(VI)- GQDs@FA@Fe-TA and MG-GQDs@FA@Fe-TA were declined by only 2.1% and 4.3% after the fifth cycle, respectively. It is possible to confirm that the Cr(VI)-GQDs@FA@Fe-TA and MG-GQDs@FA@Fe-TA could be used several times without drastically decreasing in their adsorption capabilities.

The innovative GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network was successfully prepared using a simple method to enhance the adsorption process for removal of inorganic (Cr(VI)) and organic dye (MG) pollutants. The fabricated GQDs@FA@Fe-TA was characterized by a variety of techniques to prove the surface and structure. GQDs were successfully prepared from sugarcane bagasse with 4.2 nm average particle size. The particles of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA were characterized as irregularly spherical forms in uniform distribution with particle size 37.57 nm. The assembled GQDs@FA@Fe-TA was characterized by some important loaded functional groups as amine, carboxylic acid, hydroxyl and imine groups which are capable of forcing high adsorption characters for removal of anionic pollutant as Cr(VI) and cationic pollutant as MG at pH 3.0 and 6.0, respectively. The adsorption kinetic followed the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. The effect of temperature proved an exothermic process. The kinetics and isotherms models of chromium (VI) and malachite green adsorption were followed by the pseudo-second-order and the Langmuir model. The qmax values were identified as 73.4 (Cr(VI)) and 108.1 mg g−1 (MG), respectively. The investigated GQDs@FA@Fe-TA showed excellent adsorption ability for Cr(VI) and MG, owing to the cooperative contributions of hydrogen bonding, complexation and electrostatic attraction between the two adsorbates and supramolecular network. The desorption/regeneration capacities of GQDs@FA@Fe-TA were kept constant after five cycles. The applicability and efficiency of GQs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network in removal of the two anionic and cationic pollutants from various water samples were generalized and confirmed by different real water samples as the characterized percentage recovery values were corresponding to 91.2–99.4% and 89.8–92.1% for Cr(VI) and MG, respectively. All outlined results in this study refer that the GQDs@FA@Fe-TA supramolecular network is a promising sorbent for removal of toxic heavy metal ions and cationic dyes from wastewater.

Mohamed E. Mahmoud: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Nesma A. Fekry: Data curation, Software, Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Amir M. Abdelfattah: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.
